column7: A resonator helps us hear sound more clearly by transferring vibrations to a larger object

column7: Resonators make instruments sound louder and strongly shape timbre

column7: A stylophone works because it is both a musical instrument and an electrical circuit

column7: Electric guitar pickups and related devices convert motion in a magnetic field into electrical signals
column9: Acoustic guitars depend on mechanical energy and a resonator;Electric guitars typically rely on amplification rather than a hollow body;A vibrating steel string disturbs the magnetic field around a pickup;That disturbance generates electricity in the coil of wire;The same core principle is used in microphones and many other technologies
column11: How are acoustic and electric guitars different?;What role do magnets play in pickups?;Can you find the exhibit's oldest electric guitar?

column7: Electronic instruments generate sound electronically and can be shaped in ways acoustic instruments cannot

column7: Effects pedals change an instrument's sound by altering its electrical signal

column5: How Science Brings Music to Life
column7: All musical sound begins with vibrations that travel as waves

column7: Instrument design determines both pitch possibilities and timbre

column7: Innovation and technology continually reshape musical instruments and musical styles

column7: Hearing happens when sound waves are transformed by the ear and interpreted by the brain

column7: Instrument invention combines creativity, craft, and the spread of successful ideas

column7: Mechanical instruments are early examples of automated music making

column7: This page compares two different ways European instruments create sound: vibrating air in wind instruments and vibrating strings in bowed instruments
